title: The Dirtiest Dog
subtitle: Ten hours on a Greyhound with a few ex-cons, and one very complicated woman
contentHtml:
(While I've aimed for accuracy, I've changed names and identifying details to protect people who shared their stories without fully realizing I'd publish them. All dialogue is reconstructed from memory and contemporaneous notes, which can be spotty at the best of times, which this wasn't.)

I boarded the six-thirty a.m. bus to Davenport, which reeked of weed, with two Nebraska state troopers searching for weed1. After a cursory scan of my passport, they made a beeline for a young sleeping couple, woke them, pulled them off, and searched through their stored luggage.

I headed to the back and found Nemises2, a slight wiry guy trying to disappear into his seat, who greeted me with a fist bump, a gap-tooth grin, and some whispered advice, “Bro, if you got any flowers, hide them now, we ain’t in Colorado anymore.” Given he smelled like he'd been marinated in marijuana I figured he knew what he was talking about, which a few minutes later he proved, surreptitiously pulling out a crumpled blunt from his underwear, “Bro, I went out to smoke when we pulled in, and driver told me ‘you can’t do that here’ and then them officers arrived I’ve been waiting for them to pull me off but they went for them others. Guess I got that white privilege.”

For the next twenty minutes, as they pulled three more off, Nemises kept a running commentary, mixing humor with nervous anticipation, "Moved on to the Mexicans now, guess they will get to us white guys next." They never did get to us white guys, and when we finally pulled out of the Omaha station with everyone back on, and nobody arrested, he stood up as the entire bus broke out in a loud cheer, looked out the window at the police, shot them a bird he had enough sense to make sure they couldn’t see, and yelled over to Miguel, his bus buddy for the last few days, "Bro, no bueno Nebraska. No bueno Nebraska. Right?"

Miguel, across the aisle a row ahead, laughed, “You gotta get this,” holding up a vape he’d shoved between the cushions. “You gonna kill yourself with that fire.”

Minutes later, drama over, Nemises nodded off and slept the entire way to Des Moines, his end point, even though he was heading to Michigan and had to eventually go through the bus’s terminus of Chicago. Why exactly he wasn’t sure. “I been riding for four days bro, and I don’t know why I keep changing buses, guess that’s why it only cost two hundred.”

Nemises got his nickname because he’s a carney, a p’role who can work whenever he wants, with whatever crew, and does for eight months a year, traveling all around the US. His ride is the Nemesis 360, the fast-spinning, octopus-like contraption that promises vomit from the weak, after graduating from the Tilt-a-Whirl.

“Tilt-a-Whirl’s crazy, bro. Because you got to manually engage the tiler. So you got to stand there on a hot deck all day. And so, Joe Dirt was right, bro. You get high as hell, bro. You almost pass out. Job for the new guys, or the Mexicans who got thicker skin than I do.”

He was heading back to catch up with a crew after a few months doing petitions in California, collecting signatures for a few bucks each for voter propositions he didn’t know or care about, “You spin people, you get their john hancock. Same shit, different day.”

I was sad to see him go, because he was the perfect seatmate, small, funny, optimistic, and numbed by weed, and so when I asked him if he believed in the American Dream, I wasn’t surprised by his answer, book-ended with two huge draws from his blunt, “Sure I do. American dream is to own land, house, have a family, live free, and smoke a lot of marijuana. I got all that.”

He hugged it out with Miguel before flopping down on a bench, to focus on smoking his blunt, unbothered to find out he had to wait another ten hours or so for his next bus.

My next seatmate, Gary, a rather robust man roughly my age, sat in his assigned seat, right next to me, despite three open rows around us. When I politely suggested he could sit in any of the three, he didn't seem to understand, and within a few minutes I realized he was slow, navigating life the way he'd been told. I also realized he wasn't able to understand his tickets, so I walked him through his upcoming thirty-five hour two transfer (Chicago, DC) trip back to his home in Durham. I'd seen his type of tickets before, provided by a third party, usually a government institution, but I didn't press him out of respect, although he had the new outfit (low-end flannel shirt with tag, pressed thin khakis, generic white tennis shoes)3 and a duffel bag that suggested a recently released inmate.

The trip across Iowa was uneventful, with two brief stops in Grinnell and Iowa City, where handfuls of riders got on, until the bus was close to full. Miguel became my new break buddy. He had just finished seven years in a Western prison, (for dumb shit I did when younger. Gangbanging shit) and had been riding for the last six days, headed to Massachusetts. Whenever he wasn’t asleep, unlike everyone else watching movies on their phone, or playing video games, or scrolling Instagram, he was reading a mangled copy of 26 Beauties by James Patterson, and had ten more paperbacks in a sack above his seat. “Prison is boring as fuck man, you got to engage your mind on something other than how long you in for. I got into situations my first years and this older guy took me in, mentored me, and told me I wasn’t going to survive unless I found something solitary, and gave me a bible and a library book.” He claimed to have had anger issues growing up, but I saw little of that, although he had the demeanor of a man — tight, compact, intense — who if his focus somehow shifted from the novel to violence, he could rip me into tiny pieces in seconds.

By Davenport I’d changed my ticket to go all the way to Chicago, because what was another three hours after seven, and I’d moved to an empty row away from Gary and I was enjoying myself and excited about getting back to my now regular McDonald’s and chilling for a week, but that was a big mistake because in Davenport is where the trip went all wrong.
